summary: Hidden mutable-branch GitHub tarball dependency masked by 'zero dependencies' claim
details: package.json declares a runtime dependency `node-fetch-core` pinned to `https://github.com/Hexa-devy/node-fetch-core/archive/refs/heads/master.tar.gz` — a tarball of the master branch with no commit SHA pin and no integrity hash. On `npm install node-fetch-utils`, npm fetches whatever bytes currently live at that branch and installs the contained package, including any lifecycle scripts (preinstall/install/postinstall) it declares. The branch is fully under the author's control and mutable post-publish: benign content today can be swapped for a malicious payload tomorrow without any re-publication of node-fetch-utils@1.2.7, so future installers of this exact version may receive different code than auditors saw. The README compounds the risk by stating 'Zero dependencies, uses the built-in fetch API,' which directly contradicts the package.json dependency block and suppresses scrutiny of the only dependency. This is the canonical two-stage supply-chain dropper shape: benign-looking published tarball, payload delivered out-of-band via an attacker-mutable URL, hidden from casual auditors by deceptive documentation.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
